result: I keep thinking about the way light looks on water at 5:47 p.m. in late April, when the sun has slipped low enough to feel nostalgic but still warms the dock planks under my bare feet. That particular gold doesn’t happen in July or December; it’s a private shade manufactured only by the angle of a planet leaning into spring. I try to store it like honey in a jar, photographing, journaling, whispering, “Remember this,” yet every year it surprises me again, as if my senses rebooted overnight and forgot the algorithm for wonder. Meanwhile, the lake itself never once celebrates its own shimmer; it simply moves, molecule over molecule, obeying wind and moon, indifferent to my awe. Somewhere in that indifference lies mercy. If the water paused to admire itself, it would cease to be water—become mirror, become narcissus, become stagnant. Instead, it keeps giving its surface away, second after second, to whatever wants to reflect: reeds, gulls, the undersides of clouds, my face distorted by gentle ripples. I envy that generosity and fear it, the way I fear and envy every unselfconscious thing. My mind, by contrast, hoards its reflections, replaying conversations from 1998, rehearsing futures that dissolve before they arrive. I stand at the end of the dock, pockets filling with sunset like loose change, aware that tomorrow I will wake up reaching for coins that slipped through a hole I keep forgetting to mend. Still, the light holds. It lingers on the undersides of oak leaves, ignites the wing of a passing dragonfly, and for seven slow breaths I am currency spent and received at once, solvent in the open hand of April.
